prompt: It is 1830. Daniel Webster has just delivered a devastating speech against nullification. Write to him privately — acknowledge his work, share your own views, but manage your public position carefully.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel to-webster-1830-05-27: 'I had before recd. more than one Copy from other sources; and had read the Speech with a full sense of its powerful bearing.' Must show Madison managing his public exposure: 'unwilling to make a public exhibition... it might appear obtrusive... lay me under an obligation to correct errors in other cases.' Must reveal the tactical dimension of even private correspondence. Must reference the Everett letter as a vehicle for his views without direct public engagement.
response:
My dear Mr. Webster,

I had before received more than one copy of your speech from other sources, and had read it with a full sense of its powerful bearing against the heresies which have sprung up among us. Your exposition of the nature of our Union and your vindication of the Constitution against those who would reduce it to a mere compact of sovereign states deserving no more reverence than a commercial treaty — these arguments possess a force that I confess I had not expected to see equalled in our present debates.

Yet I find myself in a position of some delicacy. Though my sentiments align entirely with your own on the fundamental question, I am unwilling to make a public exhibition of my views at this juncture. Such a course might appear obtrusive in one who has withdrawn from the active scenes of political life, and would lay me under an obligation to correct errors in other cases where my name has been invoked to support doctrines I never intended. The Virginia Resolutions of 1798, which I had the misfortune to draft, have been tortured into meanings I never contemplated, and I find that every public statement I make on these subjects draws forth a dozen new misrepresentations.

I have, however, found occasion to communicate my views more privately. Young Mr. Everett of Massachusetts has recently written to me seeking clarification of my position on the constitutional questions now agitating the country. In my response to him — which I suspect may find its way into print, as such letters often do despite one's intentions — I have endeavored to demonstrate that nullification as now preached is a doctrine wholly at variance with the Constitution we framed and ratified. The right of a single state to arrest the operation of a law of the United States within its borders would indeed put powder under the Constitution and Union and a match in the hand of every party to blow them up at pleasure.

The truth is that those who cite my Virginia Resolutions as authority for their present course have fundamentally misapprehended both the nature of those resolutions and the circumstances that gave rise to them. The resolutions were an appeal to the other states — not a declaration that Virginia possessed the unilateral power to nullify federal law. They contemplated collective action by the states against unconstitutional measures, not the isolated defiance of a single commonwealth. The distinction is crucial, though I despair of making it understood by those who find the present doctrine convenient to their purposes.

I observe with particular satisfaction your demonstration that the Constitution was ratified not by the states as corporate entities but by the people of the states acting in their highest sovereign capacity. This was indeed the theory upon which we proceeded at Philadelphia, and upon which the whole structure of our government rests. A compact between sovereign states might be dissolved at the pleasure of any party; a constitution established by the people creates a government with legitimate authority over those same people, limited only by the terms of their grant.

Your argument from the nature of government itself — that no government can exist if any part of the community may at will refuse obedience to its laws — strikes at the heart of the nullifying doctrine. I have myself observed that if this principle were sound, it would apply with equal force to the laws of the individual states. Why should the citizens of a county not possess the same right to nullify a state law they deem unconstitutional? The principle, once admitted, knows no logical stopping point short of anarchy.

I confess that I watch these developments with a heavy heart. That doctrines so subversive of all government should find advocates among men who profess attachment to our Union — that my own words should be pressed into service for purposes I never intended — these are trials I had not expected to face in my declining years. Yet your speech gives me hope that reason may yet prevail over passion, and that the Constitution may survive the assaults now made upon it.

I remain, with great respect and with entire concurrence in your constitutional principles, though I must ask you to regard this letter as confidential unless circumstances should render its publication necessary for the public good.

Your obedient servant,

James Madison
